"Hello, World!" program

"Hello, World!" program
ParadigmAny
First used1972 (Bell Labs)
PurposeIntroductory example
Common outputHello, World!

A "Hello, World!" program is generally a simple computer program which outputs (or displays) to the screen a message similar to "Hello, World!" while ignoring any input. A small piece of code in most general-purpose programming languages, this program is used to illustrate a language's basic syntax. A "Hello, World!" program is often the first written by a student of a new programming language, but such a program can also be used as a sanity check to ensure that computer software intended to compile or run source code is correctly installed, and that its operator understands how to use it.

The phrase "Hello, World!" was introduced in Brian Kernighan's 1972 paper "A Tutorial Introduction to the Language B". The first known instance of the usage of the words "hello, world" in computer literature occurred earlier, in Kernighan's internal Bell Labs memorandum documenting the B programming language. The phrase became common in the computing community after it was used in the 1978 book The C Programming Language. Since then, it has been used as an introductory program in numerous computer science textbooks.

History

The tradition of using "Hello, World!" as a test message was widely popularized by Brian Kernighan and Dennis Ritchie's 1978 book The C Programming Language. The book's second chapter opens with the program. It was also mentioned in Kernighan's earlier 1972 tutorial on the B programming language, making it one of the earliest known examples of the phrase in computing literature.

Prior to Kernighan's usage, the term "hello world" appeared in a 1967 publication by Kernighan called "A Tutorial Introduction to the Language B." The original message was chosen for its simplicity and positive character. Unlike programs that performed computation or required user input, a simple print statement demonstrates the minimum functionality needed to produce visible output from a programming environment.

The historical significance of this tradition extends beyond mere pedagogy. The "Hello, World!" program implicitly tests that the toolchain — compiler, linker, runtime, and operating system libraries — are all correctly configured and interoperating. A failed "Hello, World!" is an immediate diagnostic signal that something fundamental is broken in the development environment.

Variations by language

Hundreds of programming languages, scripting languages, markup languages, and query languages now have "Hello, World!" implementations. The variations are instructive: they reveal the syntactic weight of each language, the relationship between source code and executable form, and the conventions around standard output.

In compiled languages such as C and Go, the program demonstrates the compilation step and the linking of system libraries. In interpreted languages such as Python and Ruby, the absence of a compilation step is itself a feature demonstrated. In languages with verbose boilerplate such as Java, the program reveals class-based structure. In purely functional languages, the program may expose the handling of side effects via monads or other mechanisms.

Some programming environments — notably interactive REPLs (Read-Eval-Print Loops) — treat the concept slightly differently. In a REPL, typing an expression that evaluates to a string will print that string automatically, without an explicit print statement. This is a meaningful distinction from batch-mode execution that the "Hello, World!" example can be used to demonstrate.

C example

The canonical C implementation, as published by Kernighan and Ritchie, is widely regarded as the authoritative form of the program. It requires a single include directive, a main function, and a call to printf from the standard library.

#include <stdio.h> int main(void) { printf("Hello, World!\n"); return 0; }

The return value of main is the exit status of the process. A value of 0 conventionally indicates success. Modern compilers will often issue a warning if the return statement is omitted from a non-void function, making the explicit return 0; both correct and stylistically advised.

Compilation on a POSIX system is achieved with cc hello.c -o hello followed by ./hello. The output Hello, World! followed by a newline is sent to the standard output stream (stdout), which is typically connected to the terminal. On most systems the output is line-buffered and will appear immediately.

Python example

Python's "Hello, World!" demonstrates the language's philosophy of readability and minimal ceremony. No import statements, no boilerplate function declarations, no semicolons — just a single call to the built-in print function.

print("Hello, World!")

In Python 2, print was a statement rather than a function, meaning the parentheses were optional. Python 3 changed this, and print() is now unambiguously a function call. This is one of the few backward-incompatible changes between Python 2 and 3, and the "Hello, World!" example is sometimes used to illustrate the difference.

Running python3 hello.py from a terminal will execute the script. In interactive mode, typing print("Hello, World!") at the Python prompt produces the same result, as does simply typing the string "Hello, World!" because the REPL will display the repr of the expression.

Java example

Java's "Hello, World!" is frequently cited as an example of the language's verbosity relative to scripting languages. It requires a class declaration, a specific method signature for the entry point, and a fully qualified call to the system output stream.

public class HelloWorld { public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println("Hello, World!"); } }

The source file must be named HelloWorld.java to match the public class name — a constraint that surprises many newcomers. Compilation with javac HelloWorld.java produces a HelloWorld.class bytecode file that is executed by the Java Virtual Machine with java HelloWorld.

With the introduction of JEP 445 (Unnamed Classes and Instance Main Methods) in Java 21 as a preview feature, a simplified form of the program became possible, significantly reducing the boilerplate required. Future versions of Java may further simplify entry points for small programs, narrowing the gap with scripting languages.

JavaScript example

JavaScript's "Hello, World!" varies by environment. In a browser context, the most direct equivalent uses console.log to write to the browser's developer console. Alternatively, document.write or DOM manipulation can produce visible output on the page.

console.log("Hello, World!");

In a Node.js environment, console.log writes to stdout, making it equivalent to print in Python or printf in C. This portability across browser and server environments is one of JavaScript's defining characteristics and is demonstrated implicitly by the simple example.

Browser-based JavaScript also allows alert("Hello, World!") to produce a modal dialog, though this approach is considered poor practice in modern web development. The console.log approach is preferred in all contexts where the developer console is accessible.

Cultural impact

"Hello, World!" has transcended its origins as a programming exercise. The phrase appears in popular culture as a metaphor for first communication, debut, or announcement. Technology companies launching new products sometimes use "Hello, World!" as a nod to computing history. Books, films, and music have incorporated the phrase as a shorthand for the moment of first contact between a new entity and its environment.

In the software industry, the simplicity of the "Hello, World!" program is invoked in discussions about developer experience, framework design, and language ergonomics. A framework's "Hello, World!" — the minimum code required to produce any visible result — is a standard benchmark for assessing how much ceremony a technology imposes on beginners. Frameworks that require extensive configuration before producing output are sometimes said to have a "heavy Hello World."

The program also plays a role in technical hiring. Some interviewers ask candidates to write a "Hello, World!" program in an unfamiliar language, not to test programming skill per se but to observe how the candidate approaches an unknown environment — whether they consult documentation, ask clarifying questions, or demonstrate systematic reasoning.

In education

Educational use of "Hello, World!" begins in introductory computer science courses and extends to professional bootcamps, online tutorials, and self-directed learning resources. The program's value is not in what it teaches about algorithms or data structures but in what it teaches about the development loop: write code, compile or interpret, observe output, iterate.

For beginners, successfully running "Hello, World!" for the first time is an important psychological milestone. It confirms that the development environment is configured correctly, that the learner can navigate the necessary tools, and that code they write has a visible effect. This feedback loop — often called the "write-run-see" cycle — is foundational to programming pedagogy.

Educators have also used "Hello, World!" to introduce concepts beyond basic syntax. The choice of stdout versus stderr, the significance of exit codes, the difference between compiled and interpreted execution, and the semantics of string literals are all topics that can be explored through small variations on the classic program.

See also

A number of related topics are relevant to understanding the "Hello, World!" program in context. The following articles provide additional background on the concepts, languages, and historical figures involved:

  • Brian Kernighan — co-author of The C Programming Language
  • Dennis Ritchie — creator of the C programming language
  • B (programming language) — predecessor to C, first use of phrase
  • The C Programming Language — seminal textbook
  • Esoteric programming languages — unusual "Hello, World!" implementations
  • Software portability — related to cross-platform "Hello, World!" testing
  • Programming language comparison

References

  1. Kernighan, Brian W. (1972). "A Tutorial Introduction to the Language B". Bell Laboratories Internal Memorandum.
  2. Kernighan, Brian W.; Ritchie, Dennis M. (1978). The C Programming Language (1st ed.). Englewood Cliffs, NJ: Prentice Hall. ISBN 0-13-110163-3.
  3. Kernighan, Brian W.; Ritchie, Dennis M. (1988). The C Programming Language (2nd ed.). Englewood Cliffs, NJ: Prentice Hall. ISBN 0-13-110362-8.
  4. McIlroy, M. Douglas (1987). A Research Unix Reader: Annotated Excerpts from the Programmer's Manual. Bell Labs Technical Report.
  5. "JEP 445: Unnamed Classes and Instance Main Methods (Preview)". OpenJDK. openjdk.org. Retrieved 2024.
  6. Van Rossum, Guido (2009). "Python 3 incompatibilities — print statement to function." Python Enhancement Proposals.
  7. ECMA International (2023). ECMAScript 2023 Language Specification. ECMA-262.
  8. Abelson, Harold; Sussman, Gerald Jay (1996). Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs (2nd ed.). MIT Press. ISBN 0-262-51087-1.
